content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 27, 1992


Honorable Marian Bergerson
Senator, Thirty-Seventh District
State Capitol, Room 3063
Sacramento, CA  95814

                                        Re:   Your Request for Informal Assistance
                                              Our File No. I-92-093

Dear Senator Bergerson:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard-
ing your responsibilities under the provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act"). Since your advice request does not refer
to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request
as one for informal assistance.2

                              QUESTION

     May you, as the chairperson of the Senate's Women's Caucus
establish a separate bank account for the purpose of receiving
payments from other members of the caucus to be used for caucus
receptions, letterhead, mailers, and hearings?

                              CONCLUSION

     Since the payments made to you by members of the women's
caucus appear to be made to you for a political purpose, the
payments would be "contributions" and must be deposited into a
campaign bank account. You may have only one campaign bank
account and one controlled committee for each election.

__________________________
1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2    Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-92-093
Page 2


DISCUSSION

Contributions

Section 82015 defines "contribution" as:

[A] payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a
payment of a loan by a third party, or an
enforceable promise to make a payment except to the
extent that full and adequate consideration is
received unless it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that it is not made for political
purposes. An expenditure made at the behest of a
candidate, committee or elected officer is a
contribution to the candidate, committee or elected
officer unless full and adequate consideration is
received for making the expenditure.

Section 82025 defines "expenditure" as any payment, forgive-
ness of a loan, payment of a loan by a third party or any
enforceable promise to make a payment, unless it is clear from the
surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political
purposes. Where a payment is made or received by a candidate, the
payment is presumed to be for a political purpose. (Regulation
18215; Regulation 18225.)

Since the payments you make for the women's caucus do not
appear to be made for personal purposes unrelated to your status
as an officeholder, the payments would be "expenditures" as set
forth in the Act. (Section 82025; Regulation 18225). Since the
receipt of the payments from the other Senators for the women's
caucus do not appear to be made for personal purposes unrelated to
your status as an officeholder, the payments would be
"contributions" as set forth in the Act. (Section 82015;
Regulation 18225).

Campaign Bank Accounts

Section 85201(c) provides that all contributions or loans
made to the candidate, or to the candidate's controlled committee
shall be deposited in a single campaign bank account. Since the
payments received for the women's caucus would be contributions,
the payments must be deposited into a campaign bank account.

The Commission has interpreted Section 85201 of the Act to
permit a candidate for elective office to have only one campaign
bank account and one controlled committee for each election.
(Regulation 18521; Riddle Advice Letter, No. A-88-409.) Thus, you

3
The Commission has created a narrow exception with respect to
candidate controlled ballot measure committees which is not an
issue in this letter. (Olson Advice Letter, No. A-89-363.)

Our File No. I-92-093
Page 3


must have only one campaign bank account for each office you
intend to seek, and in addition, you may maintain a campaign bank
account established for the election to your current term of
office. Consequently, you may not have an additional campaign
bank account for the women's caucus.

Contributions with respect to the women's caucus may be
deposited in either the account established for your current term
of office, or your future campaign account.⁴ Moreover, the
campaign funds may be expended for that purpose from either
account. However, please note that Regulation 18525 provides that
as an incumbent elected officer, you must make expenditures for
mass mailings and other communications in connection with the
caucus from your reelection campaign bank account if you are
within 3 months of the election and you have filed a statement of
intention to be a candidate, a declaration of candidacy or
nomination papers, or any other documents necessary to be listed
on the ballot as a candidate for any state or local office.⁵

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.⁶

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa

enclosures


⁴ Of course the contributions received, as well as any
expenditures may be disclosable.

⁵ Regulation 18217 does permit a candidate or multiple candidates
to set up an organization as a nonprofit corporation without the
corporation becoming an additional controlled committee of the
candidate. However, the organization would need to be tax exempt
under Section 501 of the Internal Revenue Code. A copy of
Regulation 18217 has been enclosed for your information.

⁶ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.analysis:
Contributions

Section 82015 defines "contribution" as:

[A] payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent that full and adequate consideration is received unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes. An expenditure made at the behest of a candidate, committee or elected officer is a contribution to the candidate, committee or elected officer unless full and adequate consideration is received for making the expenditure.

Section 82025 defines "expenditure" as any payment, forgiveness of a loan, payment of a loan by a third party, or any enforceable promise to make a payment, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes. Where a payment is made or received by a candidate, the payment is presumed to be for a political purpose. (Regulation 18215; Regulation 18225.)

Since the payments you make for the women's caucus do not appear to be made for personal purposes unrelated to your status as an officeholder, the payments would be "expenditures" as set forth in the Act. (Section 82025; Regulation 18225). Since the receipt of the payments from the other Senators for the women's caucus do not appear to be made for personal purposes unrelated to your status as an officeholder, the payments would be "contributions" as set forth in the Act. (Section 82015; Regulation 18225).

Campaign Bank Accounts

Section 85201(c) provides that all contributions or loans made to the candidate, or to the candidate's controlled committee shall be deposited in a single campaign bank account. Since the payments received for the women's caucus would be contributions, the payments must be deposited into a campaign bank account.

The Commission has interpreted Section 85201 of the Act to permit a candidate for elective office to have only one campaign bank account and one controlled committee for each election.3 (Regulation 18521; Riddle Advice Letter, No. A-88-409.) Thus, you

3 The Commission has created a narrow exception with respect to candidate controlled ballot measure committees which is not an issue in this letter. (Olson Advice Letter, No. A-89-363.)

must have only one campaign bank account for each office you intend to seek, and in addition, you may maintain a campaign bank account established for the election to your current term of office. Consequently, you may not have an additional campaign bank account for the women's caucus.

Contributions with respect to the women's caucus may be deposited in either the account established for your current term of office, or your future campaign account.4 Moreover, the campaign funds may be expended for that purpose from either account. However, please note that Regulation 18525 provides that as an incumbent elected officer, you must make expenditures for mass mailings and other communications in connection with the caucus from your reelection campaign bank account if you are within 3 months of the election and you have filed a statement of intention to be a candidate, a declaration of candidacy or nomination papers, or any other documents necessary to be listed on the ballot as a candidate for any state or local office.5
